item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something something BIG YEET

Thanks for the crit updoot :>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rolled a 20. Critical upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Containment makes sense for a D&D player, but isn't sufficiently explained how it came to be as it is.

I'm personally a fan of shorter, snappier articles, so I try to keep the containment relatively minimalistic (with a focus on straight instruction-giving, over descriptions as to the whys of everything). If there's a way to introduce clearer explanations for the containment in the description in a way that doesn't bog down the reading, what do you think should be added?

How does tentative interaction with non-interactive elements looks like?

I thought the phrase "only certain items in the location can be interacted with" implied that everything else is just fixed in place. Do you think a specific footnote or additional sentence clarifying this needs to be added?

Why there is no description of the interior of the castle aside its general architecture?

The Foundation, as a scientific organization, didn't find anything of note that would merit describing it in greater detail for this particular documentation.

It would also be interesting to have the Foundation at least try to figure out where to find the rules

There aren't any rules present in the castle layout itself. The SCP-2448-1 are basically just parroting things that the anomaly has "learned" from the players.

My impression is that it begs for an exploration log

What do you think should happen in such an exploration log? Again though, I personally am not a huge fan of excessively long articles that are bogged down with excess supplementary content; if I did write an exploration log for this, it would be on a separate page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a D&D GM myself, I loved this skip. At first I was a little angry at the stupidity of the game, but at the end I got the gist of the learning mind behind the game, and it all fitted in place for me.

I like, for sure, but first I do have some issues, and I'll decide latter if I will upvote or downvote this (for the sake of improvement).

1- The Containment makes sense for a D&D player, but isn't sufficiently explained how it came to be as it is. Why 5 person teams? What happened with "single-class" teams? How does unexpected weaponry, or unorthodox approaches in general, make it easier? Those things are for efficiency, safety, or the goal is to get more data on how the entity adapts to "play-styles"?

2- How does tentative interaction with non-interactive elements looks like? Is the feature unexpected fixed in place, or even in mid-air? Or is it intangible? Does it escapes the person's grasp by obvious movement? Or does it seems to dodge by sheer luck and happenstance?

3- Why there is no description of the interior of the castle aside its general architecture? Is even this castle fixed, or does it changes configuration from time to time? The main structure of the adventure seems too featureless to me, I only know that there are critters in the fields and a throne room in the castle, until NPCs start to guard rooms at random. It would also be interesting to have the Foundation at least try to figure out where to find the rules (and then it could be introduced the hint that they already got it as being D&D, it just wasn't relevant to tell in the documentation).

4- My impression is that it begs for an exploration log. The core of this skip is that it interacts with the Foundation personnel, and we have very little of the actual interaction. We get that it learns from this interaction, but it is told, not shown.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… I like the first link, but it seems kind of randomly placed, in that it feels like more of a shoehorn than a natural note made by the in-universe author of the document. I'll make an edit myself implementing the crosslink though!

I'm not as much a fan of the second link, so I'll be declining it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Reading this article reminded me of two different anomalies, which I propose crosslinks to.

Firstly, this is a D&D game come to life. As such, the IRL D&D campaign SCP could be linked in one of the moments in which this connection is most obvious. Proposing editing this:

Markers near the property state the location as "The Village of Gyfax".

To:

Markers near the property state the location as "The Village of Gyfax".

Additionally, reading this article reminded me of SCP-1384, which is another medieval-themed article featuring a cosmic game with an unknown set of rules. (Which, according to the author, is somewhat of a more complicated cosmic game of chess come to life) This is thematically similar, and could be linked. Changing:

When SCP-2448 entities are questioned about aspects of the location, they simply respond that the questioner should "read the rules".

To:

When SCP-2448 entities are questioned about aspects of the location, they simply respond that the questioner should "read the rules".

(Specifically to the supplement of that SCP, which describes the rules of the anomaly.)

If anyone has any other idea, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scorpion451 has it right.

I didn't like the idea of tiny humanoid creatures playing a game in the castle though.

Not sure where you're getting this. There aren't any tiny humanoids in this article? Not that I remember, anyway…

It seems like they point out there must be rules the Foundation must follow to play the game correctly, however what rules?

Consult the DM guide.

More replies
hide

And why do the humanoid creatures get more intelligent and capable of more movement from time to time.

The "game" is learning.

However I think it does lack a bit of more information to grow the suspicion and unpredictability of where the story could go after the Addendum 4.

I'm not sure if "suspicious" and "unpredictable" would contribute to the mood I was aiming for here.

we have no explanation to why its looks are of a 13th century English castle in Switzerland or to why it is called "The village of Gyfax". Same with Guillaume the guild master and to why the written languages on the plates being French German etc.

Gyfax is a nod to Gary Gygax. The rest might just be Rule of Cool; I'll have to dig up the notes I made when I wrote this.

Or perhaps removing certain aspects that cause it to sound like different ideas that do not work so well together

Granted, most of the seemingly random stuff is Easter eggs for the D&D players/fans out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Everything you're listing here has a single explanation: it's a D&D campaign come to life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I didn't get the whole thing quite right, however I'll toss here my opinion about it, with no intentions of starting a discussion.
I liked the way it was written and the whole castle concept with the containment procedures being very attractive to my liking.
I didn't like the idea of tiny humanoid creatures playing a game in the castle though. It seems like they point out there must be rules the Foundation must follow to play the game correctly, however what rules? And why do the humanoid creatures get more intelligent and capable of more movement from time to time. I think we're lacking a bit of background behind this story that could cover up the reason why this was at first classified as an SCP, maybe there were sightings of these creatures at night, maybe voices could be heard, I don't know… However I think it does lack a bit of more information to grow the suspicion and unpredictability of where the story could go after the Addendum 4. As I read in some comments above, as soon as the description starts, we have no explanation to why its looks are of a 13th century English castle in Switzerland or to why it is called "The village of Gyfax". Same with Guillaume the guild master and to why the written languages on the plates being French German etc.

Summing it up. It seems a little confusing and needs a little more adding explanation to it. Or perhaps removing certain aspects that cause it to sound like different ideas that do not work so well together could make it more comprehensible and a better article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I was waiting for the link to Wondertainment's Pretendo system, a la the Eskimo Joe SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
are you suggesting that there should be more added to the castle?

+ show block
– hide block

Probably not a good idea. You have like 58 upvotes and eight downvotes as of this posting, so clearly you are doing something right (you also have a ton of stuff people (including me) really like already, for that matter).

The behavior of the swarms is a feature of the SCP (tiiiiny reference to the phrase "game night"), and I don't see why it has to be considered a reference elsewhere…

+ show block
– hide block

It felt like one. Like it was very familiar but I couldn't put my finger on it. I think maybe there's something in Ravenloft like this, actually.

I kind of feel like you're digging for Easter eggs that aren't there?

+ show block
– hide block

Perhaps. The ones that I am aware of being there seem either kind of obvious (owlbears) or off (hexes).

I'm pretty sure at least one edition of D&D incorporates hex maps.

+ Crusty Grognard Blathering
- No thank you, I don't need to hear about your character.

Not, AFAIK, as a non-optional core rule. Early editions used the Chainmail miniatures wargaming rules which are gridless. After that it's been 5' square grids all the way.

Overland movement (hex-crawls) used hexes in the older editions that had them, but those hexes would never fit in a room.

Now 3.5 and Pathfinder have optional hex rules in non-core supplements; Unearthed Arcana and Ultimate Combat respectively, but I've never played with anybody that uses them. When I think "RPG" and "hex grid" I don't think D&D; I think GURPS but this isn't the "Village of Jackson".

Again, you're picking up on way more references than I had in mind when originally writing this, so I'm not sure what to do about that.

As I said you probably shouldn't do anything…

+ Curmudgeonly Grumbling
- And pull up your damn pants!

…except say "Poor sirpudding, I'm sorry you got your sense of humor shot off in the war1. Thank you for your service.2"; since your stuff in general (and this in particular) is well received by the general audience.

I think in general I don't like scips that are pop culture references at all, so this is very nearly an automatic downvote for me based entirely on it's core concept. That said, I think that it might work if it were subtler: no "Village of Gygax", and so on (I'm actually surprised you didn't use "Village of Homlet"; not that would have helped much).

I might like it, if the "DM" acted more like a real DM, instead of a really bad computer game. Going through common stages of development (railroading, antagonistic, and so on) arriving at some mature stage of running fun games with good narratives. but without overtly saying that this is a "living RPG".

As it is it seems really confused as to the difference between tabletop games and computer games.

I still might not like it that way, though. It would have a pretty high bar to clear as a main-list scip to get over it being D&D fan-fic in the first place, IMO. YMMV, of course, and clearly does.

Footnotes
1. The VA gave me a prosthetic sense of humor. It's alright, but is definitely a little mechanical.
2. Don't actually do this. I don't like it. I am a grumpy mean 'ole crazy Marine. Carry on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Again, I didn't have Castle Greyhawk in mind at all when writing this. Also, I'm not sure where you're going with all the references… are you suggesting that there should be more added to the castle?

The behavior of the swarms is a feature of the SCP (tiiiiny reference to the phrase "game night"), and I don't see why it has to be considered a reference elsewhere… I kind of feel like you're digging for Easter eggs that aren't there?

Then it's not really just D&D then, is it?

I'm pretty sure at least one edition of D&D incorporates hex maps. It makes it easier to move diagonally or something?

I also think it's way too silly/pop culture reference-y to not be a -J skip.

Again, you're picking up on way more references than I had in mind when originally writing this, so I'm not sure what to do about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nothing in the article is a reference to Castle Greyhawk. D&D has swarms.

Sure it does. It also has Flumphs and Githyanki. I don't see either of those in this article. The whole "swarms that appear from nowhere at night and then are gone in the morning thing" strikes me as the kind of gonzo thing that was in a lot of Gygax adventures, and especially Castle Greyhawk. If it isn't, I'm not sure what it's a reference to at all.

Maybe the castle encountered someone who likes hexes more than squares and agreed with them.

Then it's not really just D&D then, is it?

The tiles fit in a room. AD&D overland hexes were like 10 square miles, IIRC. Tiling a room is a normal battle map.

Anyway, clearly I just don't get it. I also think it's way too silly/pop culture reference-y to not be a -J skip. Sorry, my sense of humor was shot off in the war. Carry on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suffice to say, the castle (and its consciousness) is not human. It still has its tastes and preferences, though. ;)

Nothing in the article is a reference to Castle Greyhawk. D&D has swarms.

Maybe the castle encountered someone who likes hexes more than squares and agreed with them. Besides, tactical combat isn't all that happens in D&D.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, I mostly get that. It's a bad DM. It acts more like a computer game than any human bad DM though. Bad human DMs railroad, or have Mary Sue DMPCs or whatever. They don't react mechanically to stimuli.

Also what are the swarms a reference to? Is this something from Castle Greyhawk (I never played it)?

Why hexes? D&D uses squares for tactical combat. If it was the Village of Jackson, hexes would make sense. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…Have you read through the other comments here?

Spoilers?
It's D&D.

Also, in regards to your other statement, the castle is still learning how to do the GMing. The NPCs are an extension of the castle's consciousness, not a distinct manifestation, and the castle is trying to do its best based on how the players act. The Foundation doesn't know if it uses other methods to change up gameplay, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been playing tabletop rpgs since 1986 and I don't get this. Are these specific references to things in Castle Greyhawk?

Generally NPCs in tabletop games can talk about anything at all, because there's a real human (the GM) playing them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm half expecting people to start hallucinating experience bars XD

I LIKE THIS IDEA.

inb4 "my XP is bigger than yours" jokes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get most of the references, but that's probably just because i've never played tabletop games before. I'm half expecting people to start hallucinating experience bars XD
Anyway, got my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not so sure… In the context of testing they use D class don't they?

No D-Class involved here, because the Foundation wants people with Foundation experimental/historical/SCIENCE!!! experience navigating this place. Introduce logic, make the game logical by the Foundation's rules, control the game. The castle doesn't really like the hard science, though… ;)

They don't let test subjects read containment procedures anyway

It was my belief that they'd be given knowledge of whatever containment is necessary/relevant to complete the task, plus a mission brief.

so listing it here shouldn't have a direct effect on its manifestations, should it?

The Foundation doesn't know if that will be the case. They want to find out more before they send in D&D-savvy people, but the game keeps surprising the observers before they can nail down solid theories. I can totally foresee an event in the future when they figure out how the castle works, and then send in a couple Foundation people who are/were DMs saying, "go nuts".

Honestly, I'm making it sound like a bigger deal than I think it is, and I think it's a fine entry overall, I'm probably just picking nits.

Nah, I like it when people talk about SCPs in depth. I think the hardest part of being an author is modulating information between both reader enjoyment and in-universe believability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not so sure… In the context of testing they use D class don't they? If they know, but won't say, that doesn't eliminate the possibility that someone will figure it out and say "I wonder if there's any ogre magi!?" if a test subject recognizes the trends.

They don't let test subjects read containment procedures anyway, so listing it here shouldn't have a direct effect on its manifestations, should it? Whereas I think such a pervasive parallel to a known and we'll established fictional universe would be important descriptive info.

Honestly, I'm making it sound like a bigger deal than I think it is, and I think it's a fine entry overall, I'm probably just picking nits.

Edit: Upon rereading I see I forgot that agents are used in testing as well (or more accurately, exploration). In that case the info could still be present, but expunged/blackboxed, I feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
but it's similarly jarring to read a whole entry describing real life D&D written by scientists (who are almost invariably nerds to varying degrees) without someone just coming out and saying "hey this whole thing is a real life RPG".

Actually, I'd considered this. Here's what my thought process was…

Potential spoilers
potential spoilers

The Foundation knows that this is a D&D-esque RPG. However, seeing as the castle seems to be learning based on the players, and no one knows exactly how it's learning, there's no outright "hey this is an RPG expect dragons" line because there's probably a decent chance that the moment they send someone in whose mind is full of terrifying creatures, the castle will be like "oh man I love that" and BAM you've got a hydra running around.

After all, it made proto-owlbears. God forbid the Foundation send someone in who's expecting a tarrasque.

Edit: Anyway, my thought process was that the Foundation is trying not to prime any of the people sent in until they know more about what the castle is capable of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked this. I've been playing D&D since I was 8 so I'm an easy sell. I have to say though, the Foundation would quickly and easily know that this skip follows the general rules and traditions of a role playing game, if not specifically D&D, and would likely note as such in the description and (if pertinent) containment procedures.

I know it would detract from the novelty of the article quite a bit to come out and say it from go, but it's similarly jarring to read a whole entry describing real life D&D written by scientists (who are almost invariably nerds to varying degrees) without someone just coming out and saying "hey this whole thing is a real life RPG". It almost seems like willful ignorance, and irresponsible to NOT mention it in the context of providing any relevant information.

Nonetheless, my criticism really only points to a minor catch-22, so upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I respond
thus

Everything is made of Styrofoam like in a larp

Not quite! The material is actually plastic. Also, read the footnote. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading through again I found

a few more
– hide block

The -1s are drawing hex grids which can be used as a map in some rpgs
Everything is made of Styrofoam like in a larp
As I had to edit it into my last post I'll mention the owlbears again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's at least two more Easter eggs. ;)

Think of...
the details.

D&D uses more than numbers and actions… think solid. Physical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No I have not I just picked this name because i like Pandas and vorpal things but it does sound cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
/me peeeeeers at your username

friend

have you perchance heard of

the illustrious pandamanta

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love RPG's and d&d so an SCP that's a " real life" d&d is cool to me i'd love to see more of what this thing comes up with past the owl bears as well +1ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I found
– hide block

Gyfax is close to Gygax
Variety of skill group works better because you don't want a party of all fighters
Guild master can be abbreviated to GM just as game master can.
Just before the crit hit is mention of sudden clumsiness the crit miss
5 man team is the default party size in 4th ed d&d (maybe other editions also)
Edit: And how dare I forget the owlbear

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Natural crit, yes. Can you spot the other Easter eggs? ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the first notable example involved Agent Tai Yang Shen managing to eliminate a swarm of bats using a single lit torch.

He clearly rolled a Natural 20.
And then I upvoted.

item.title: You rolled a 20 when writing this.
item.content:encoded: I think i even found all the easter eggs! well done well done in deed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People keep talking about how there's owlbears in this skip, but I can't seem to find the reference.
Can anyone point me in the right direction?
Also this is a pretty interesting concept. I'm upvoting it.
EDIT: Never mind, I just found the reference.
I'm stupid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Proto-owlbears. The game hasn't really figured them out yet. And the Foundation isn't really sure exactly what the game is using as inspiration for its updates.

I think this might just be too silly for an upvote.

I can understand the sentiment. Originally there were a lot fewer references, but more reviewers felt the backstory was much harder to pick up on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You put owlbears in your RPG skip. I'm not sure how I feel about this.

Though reading Petrograd's comment, changes aside, I think this might just be too silly for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anything that would prevent the SCP from being best described as simply "A place where real life becomes D&D". Like, if I went through all of the elements of the SCP and tried to list what they most obviously draw from, the list would basically be Dungeons and Dragons, roleplaying games that take heavy inspiration from D&D, and roleplaying video games.

Now if there were some other elements thrown in there — like, off the top of my head, Daevites — in a way that doesn't horribly clash with what's already there and makes sense (or feels like it could), that would actually pique my interest.

You could also cut its length in half and then I could hardly complain that it only does one thing, but then you'd just have a worse SCP-1024.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of curiosity, what would an example of far enough beyond be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can incorporate that. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's banking too hard on tabletop fantasy roleplaying tropes for me to really dig. I mean, yes, that is the concept, but it doesn't go far enough BEYOND that imagery/atmosphere for me to enjoy it as its own thing. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, that makes sense. I would've liked to see a bit more explicit stakes than that, ("and disappear." not sounding very urgent or momentous), but I understand when part of a story gets bloated/weird enough to detract from the article.

Edit 2: Even something like, "will cross the boundaries of the castle grounds and disperse. Any further movements of these creatures are unknown." or "… untraceable."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
at which point they will cross the boundaries of the castle grounds and disappear.

Where do they disappear to? Is there any way to know for sure they're gone for good, or actually gone at all? Nothing crosses the boundaries of the grounds unless the quest/objective/what-have-you isn't completed. It was something inherently puzzling, so the Foundation decided to continue to continue investigating it. Thing is, each successive investigation resulted in more questions.

The initial drafts actually had the pest creatures invading nearby areas and causing crop and structural damage, but that part got long and nonsensical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's one thing I don't quite understand, and that's kind of dampening the rest of it for me: Why does the Foundation "play" at all?

From the description, it seems as if the anomalous creatures that appear just disappear the next morning. I could understand doing it for research, but right now it reads as if the majority of the containment procedures are meant to prevent something that has absolutely no consequences if ignored.

Am I missing or misreading something? If not, it's kind of a plot hole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
where there are owlbears

there must also be duckbunnies

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Suggestion; have the enemies drop currency which can be used with the NPCs.

The game needs to figure out what currency is first. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually liked the previous version a bit better, but this is still good. +1

Suggestion; have the enemies drop currency which can be used with the NPCs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first, didn't really get what was going on, it all seemed a bit random. Then I read the "tessellating hexagonal patterns" being drawn, went "bloody hell you're a genius" and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how, as a result of trying to be better at being a game, exposure to the Foundation is making it more and more focused on rules enforcement over storytelling.

EDIT: It has my upvote either way, but this skip also hits my weakness for owlbears. Even for proto-owlbears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it.

item.title: Author Notes
item.content:encoded:
Many, many, many thanks to Athena_Grey, Goomy, lurkd, Fae, TwistedGears, MacLeod, Ireul, kaktus, ProcyonLotor, MrAnakinSpecter, tahugamin, Live2Die, BobDragonslayer|2, Soulless, Wogglebug, MisterFlames, Vince, Silberescher, Dexanote, Roget, Dr_Mann, anqxyr, and Harmonious for reading this over in chat. Special thanks to Voct for giving me ideas for background references!

Credit goes to Lurkd and Riemann for the salted shoes and the technical-sounding phrases.

You enter a room with potential spoilers
A spoiler attacks!
  • For the record, the only tabletop RPG I've played consistently was Heroscape, back when it was a thing. Ranged characters FTW.
  • D&D fans, see if you can spot all the Easter eggs.
  • This SCP is effectively trying to improve itself based on player interaction.
  • At first, all it could make were regular animals and bugs. When Foundation staff started to play, it started to learn.
  • Thing is, it adapts/improves faster than the Foundation can get satisfactory documentation written. Hence the addenda pew pew pew more info every quest.
  • The Foundation doesn't know what it's capable of, nor are they certain what the thing wants.
  • So they keep playing.

Also, everyone should go watch the Acquisitions Incorporated campaigns. Chris Perkins is my hero. Perkins DM is best DM.
